skill.name: codebase-design
skill.description: Guides software teams in designing deep modules with small interfaces, clear seams, useful adapters, and testable architecture.
skill.summary: Shared vocabulary and review process for improving module interfaces and codebase structure.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: The reported weak-cryptography hits are false positives from ordinary prose, Markdown filenames, and design vocabulary. The reported external command hits are Markdown code fences, not executable shell or Ruby backticks, and no prompt injection or data exfiltration evidence was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: Static analysis matched words in Markdown prose and filenames, not cryptographic algorithm usage. No hashing, encryption, or credential handling code was present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are Markdown instruction documents. The matched lines contain architecture vocabulary, headings, links, or prose rather than cryptographic operations.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Ruby Or Shell Backtick Execution
low_findings.description: Static analysis matched Markdown code fence delimiters. These backticks format diagrams and examples, and they are not shell execution syntax.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each cited location is a Markdown fence around documentation examples. There is no runtime script, command invocation, or user input execution path.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Pattern
low_findings.description: Static analysis matched a TypeScript testing example about side effects. The line does not inspect the host system or collect environment data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is inside a documentation example for testability. It contains no system command, platform query, network request, or filesystem access.

content.value_statement: Large interfaces make code harder to change, test, and explain. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code evaluate module depth, seams, adapters, and test surfaces.
actual_capabilities: Defines a shared vocabulary for modules, interfaces, seams, adapters, depth, leverage, and locality.
Helps identify shallow modules and opportunities to hide complexity behind smaller interfaces.
Classifies dependencies for refactoring, including in-process, local-substitutable, owned remote, and external services.
Guides test strategy toward observable behavior through the module interface.
Supports comparing multiple alternative interface designs before selecting a direction.
limitations: It provides design guidance, not automated static analysis or code transformation.
It depends on accurate project context from the user or repository.
It does not replace domain knowledge from maintainers.
It may be too abstract for small one-off scripts.

use_cases.title: Refactor a Shallow Module
use_cases.description: Find where repeated caller logic can move behind a smaller and more useful interface.

use_cases.title: Plan a Testable Architecture Change
use_cases.description: Choose seams and adapters so behavior can be tested through stable module interfaces.

use_cases.title: Compare Interface Options
use_cases.description: Generate several design alternatives and compare them by depth, locality, and seam placement.

prompt_templates.title: Review Module Depth
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the codebase-design skill to review this module. Identify its interface, implementation, seam, and whether it is deep or shallow.

prompt_templates.title: Find Refactoring Opportunities
prompt_templates.prompt: Use codebase-design to find shallow modules in this area. Recommend one deepening opportunity with the smallest useful interface.

prompt_templates.title: Design Testable Seams
prompt_templates.prompt: Use codebase-design and classify each dependency. Propose seams, adapters, and tests that verify behavior through the new interface.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Multiple Interfaces
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Design It Twice process. Produce three different interfaces, compare depth and locality, then recommend the strongest design.

output_examples.input: Review the checkout module for design issues.
output_examples.output: The review identifies the checkout interface, explains why caller logic makes it shallow, and proposes a smaller order-processing interface.

output_examples.input: Help me make this payment flow easier to test.
output_examples.output: The output classifies dependencies, recommends a payment port, and describes tests that use a local adapter through the same interface.

output_examples.input: Compare possible interfaces for importing customer data.
output_examples.output: The answer presents several interface designs, explains trade-offs, and recommends the option with the best leverage and locality.

best_practices: Provide the relevant files, callers, tests, and dependencies before asking for a design recommendation.
Ask the assistant to name the module, interface, seam, adapter, leverage, and locality explicitly.
Compare at least two interface options before committing to a major refactor.
anti_patterns: Do not add seams unless two real adapters or behaviors justify them.
Do not test private implementation details when behavior can be tested through the interface.
Do not treat a TypeScript interface alone as the full design interface.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps design modules with smaller interfaces, better seams, clearer adapters, and stronger test surfaces.

faq.question: Can it change my code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It guides design decisions and can help plan edits, but it is not an automated refactoring tool.

faq.question: When should I use it?
faq.answer: Use it when a module is hard to test, has many callers, or exposes too much implementation detail.

faq.question: Does it work with Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is written as tool-neutral guidance for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What context should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide the module, its callers, dependencies, tests, and the reason the current design is painful.

faq.question: Is this only for TypeScript?
faq.answer: No. The examples include TypeScript, but the module design vocabulary applies across programming languages.
